articles.headline: Group Captain Shubhanshu Shukla Becomes Second Indian in Space Aboard Axiom Mission 4
articles.explainer:
• Group Captain Shubhanshu Shukla made history as the second Indian citizen to journey into outer space, after Rakesh Sharma in 1984.
• He served as a mission specialist aboard Axiom Mission 4 (Ax-4), a private spaceflight to the International Space Station (ISS).
• The mission, which includes astronauts from other nations, successfully launched from the Kennedy Space Center in the United States.
• Shukla's participation underscores India's growing collaboration in international space ventures and its aspiration to send Indian astronauts on future missions.
• This achievement highlights India's progress in space technology and human spaceflight capabilities, aligning with the Gaganyaan mission objectives.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Space technology
Awareness in the fields of Space
prelimsPoints: Shubhanshu Shukla is the second Indian to go to space (first was Rakesh Sharma in 1984).
He flew as a Group Captain on Axiom Mission 4 (Ax-4).
Ax-4 is a private mission to the International Space Station (ISS).
The launch took place from Kennedy Space Center, USA.
Axiom Space is a private company facilitating commercial space missions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's participation in international commercial space missions for its own space program (Gaganyaan).
Analyze the role of private sector in global space exploration and India's potential.
Evaluate the geopolitical implications of India's growing presence in space, including collaboration and competition.
Examine the scientific and technological spin-offs from human spaceflight missions for India.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of an astronaut with the Indian flag on a spacesuit, with a rocket launching in the background.
articles.trivia: The first Indian-origin person to go to space was Kalpana Chawla, but Rakesh Sharma was the first Indian citizen. Shubhanshu Shukla is the second Indian citizen.

articles.headline: Devastating Flash Floods in Himachal Pradesh Claim Lives, Leave Many Missing
articles.explainer:
• Heavy rainfall triggered severe flash floods and landslides across several districts of Himachal Pradesh.
• At least three fatalities have been confirmed, with rescue operations underway for 11 individuals reported missing.
• The floods have caused extensive damage to infrastructure, including roads, bridges, and residential areas, disrupting normal life.
• Authorities have issued high alert warnings, advising residents and tourists to avoid vulnerable areas and initiating relief measures.
• The incident highlights the increasing vulnerability of the Himalayan region to extreme weather events, exacerbated by climate change and unplanned development.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena
Disaster and disaster management
Changes in critical geographical features
prelimsPoints: Flash floods are characterized by their rapid onset and short duration, often caused by intense rainfall.
Himachal Pradesh is part of the Western Himalayas, prone to geological instability and heavy monsoonal rains.
The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) is the apex body for disaster management in India.
NDRF (National Disaster Response Force) plays a crucial role in search and rescue operations.
mainsPoints: Analyze the causes of increasing frequency and intensity of flash floods in the Himalayan region, including climate change and anthropogenic factors.
Discuss the challenges in disaster preparedness and response in mountainous terrains like Himachal Pradesh.
Suggest comprehensive strategies for disaster risk reduction and mitigation in vulnerable regions, focusing on early warning systems, infrastructure resilience, and community participation.
Evaluate the role of land-use planning and environmental regulations in preventing such disasters.
articles.imageDescription: Satellite image showing heavy cloud cover over the Himalayan region, indicative of monsoonal activity.
articles.trivia: Himachal Pradesh is often referred to as 'Dev Bhoomi' (Land of Gods) due to its numerous ancient temples and picturesque natural beauty.

articles.headline: Thirty-Five Nepali Youths Rescued from Captivity in Uttarakhand
articles.explainer:
• A joint operation by the Nepali Embassy and Indian authorities successfully rescued 35 Nepali youths from captivity in Kashipur, Uttarakhand.
• The youths were reportedly held under exploitative conditions, highlighting issues of human trafficking and illegal labor practices.
• The coordinated effort demonstrates strong bilateral cooperation between India and Nepal on humanitarian and security matters.
• Investigations are ongoing to apprehend the perpetrators and dismantle the human trafficking network responsible.
• This incident underscores the need for enhanced cross-border vigilance and legal frameworks to combat human trafficking and protect vulnerable migrant workers.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood relations
Internal security challenges
Social justice and protection of vulnerable sections
keyTerms: Schengen Area (for comparison of open borders)
prelimsPoints: India and Nepal share an open border, facilitating movement but also posing challenges for security.
Human trafficking is a transnational organized crime.
The Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) coordinates anti-human trafficking efforts in India.
The Immoral Traffic (Prevention) Act, 1956 (ITPA) is the primary legislation against trafficking for sexual exploitation.
mainsPoints: Examine the socio-economic factors that make Nepali youths vulnerable to human trafficking in India.
Discuss the challenges and successes of India-Nepal bilateral cooperation in combating cross-border crimes like human trafficking.
Suggest policy measures and institutional reforms required to strengthen anti-human trafficking efforts in India, focusing on prevention, protection, and prosecution.
Analyze the role of international organizations and NGOs in addressing human trafficking in South Asia.
articles.imageDescription: Image of Indian and Nepali flags intertwined, symbolizing bilateral cooperation.
articles.trivia: The India-Nepal border is one of the most open international borders in the world, allowing free movement of people.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Directs Review of Electoral Bond Scheme, Citing Transparency Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India has issued directives to the central government for a comprehensive review of the Electoral Bond Scheme.
• The court expressed concerns regarding the scheme's opacity and its potential impact on electoral transparency and fair political funding.
• This ruling comes amidst ongoing debates about the anonymity of political donations and the need for greater accountability in party funding.
• The government has been asked to consider amendments that ensure donor identity disclosure while balancing privacy concerns.
• The decision is a significant step towards reinforcing democratic principles and ensuring a level playing field in elections.
gsSubTopics: Separation of powers
Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
prelimsPoints: Electoral Bonds were introduced in 2017 to provide a mechanism for political funding.
The scheme allows individuals and corporate entities to donate to political parties anonymously.
The Supreme Court had previously heard petitions challenging the scheme's constitutionality.
The Election Commission of India (ECI) has also raised concerns about electoral transparency.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the Electoral Bond Scheme in light of principles of transparency, accountability, and free and fair elections.
Discuss the arguments for and against anonymous political donations in a democratic setup.
Analyze the role of the Supreme Court as a guardian of the Constitution in electoral matters.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for political funding that promote transparency while ensuring ease of donation.
articles.imageDescription: Gavel and Indian Constitution book on a courtroom desk.
articles.trivia: The first general elections in India in 1951-52 were conducted over several months due to logistical challenges.

articles.headline: India and Japan Deepen Strategic Ties with Focus on Indo-Pacific Security
articles.explainer:
• India and Japan held their annual Strategic Dialogue in New Delhi, reaffirming their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defense cooperation, maritime security, and regional stability in the face of evolving geopolitical challenges.
• Both nations emphasized collaboration on critical and emerging technologies, supply chain resilience, and infrastructure development in the region.
• The dialogue also touched upon multilateral cooperation within forums like the Quad and G20, strengthening their comprehensive strategic partnership.
• This engagement reflects a concerted effort to counter regional assertiveness and promote a rules-based international order.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India and Japan are members of the Quad grouping, along with the USA and Australia.
Japan is a key partner in India's Act East Policy.
Both countries share concerns regarding maritime security in the Indo-Pacific.
Defense exercises like 'Dharma Guardian' and 'JIMEX' are conducted between India and Japan.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic convergence between India and Japan in the Indo-Pacific region and its implications for regional security.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in deepening defense and economic cooperation between India and Japan.
Evaluate the role of bilateral and multilateral forums like Quad in shaping the geopolitical landscape of the Indo-Pacific.
Examine how India's 'Act East' policy aligns with Japan's strategic interests in the region.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan displayed together in a formal setting.
articles.trivia: Japan is a major investor in India's infrastructure projects, including the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail corridor.

articles.headline: RBI Holds Repo Rate Steady Amidst Persistent Inflationary Pressures
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) decided to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged at its current level.
• This decision reflects the RBI's cautious approach, prioritizing inflation control despite calls for economic growth stimulus.
• The MPC highlighted persistent inflationary pressures, particularly from food prices and global commodity markets, as key concerns.
• The central bank reaffirmed its commitment to withdrawing accommodation gradually to ensure inflation aligns with the target band.
• Analysts anticipate continued vigilance from the RBI, with future rate decisions dependent on evolving inflation trajectory and global economic conditions.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary policy
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is responsible for setting the benchmark interest rate (repo rate) in India.
The primary objective of the RBI's monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation targeting framework in India aims for CPI inflation of 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
mainsPoints: Analyze the dilemma faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and economic growth in the current global and domestic scenario.
Discuss the effectiveness of monetary policy tools, particularly the repo rate, in managing inflationary pressures in India.
Evaluate the impact of global commodity prices and supply chain disruptions on India's domestic inflation.
Examine the challenges in achieving the inflation target and propose measures for better coordination between fiscal and monetary policies.
articles.imageDescription: RBI logo with Indian currency notes in the background.
articles.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

articles.headline: National Digital Health Mission Expands Reach, Focuses on Tier-2 and Tier-3 Cities
articles.explainer:
• The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM), now known as Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM), announced its expanded rollout to Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities.
• The expansion aims to bridge the healthcare accessibility gap by leveraging digital technologies for health records, teleconsultations, and seamless service delivery.
• Key components include Health ID generation, Healthcare Professionals Registry (HPR), and Health Facility Registry (HFR).
• The initiative seeks to create an integrated digital health infrastructure, improving efficiency and transparency in the healthcare system.
• Challenges remain in ensuring digital literacy, data privacy, and interoperability across diverse healthcare providers.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Development processes and the development industry—the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
prelimsPoints: The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) was renamed Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM).
It aims to create a national digital health ecosystem.
Key features include Health ID, Health Facility Registry, and Healthcare Professionals Registry.
The mission is under the National Health Authority (NHA).
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to transform healthcare delivery in India, especially in remote and underserved areas.
Analyze the challenges involved in implementing a nationwide digital health infrastructure, including data privacy, digital divide, and cybersecurity.
Evaluate the role of technology in improving healthcare accessibility, affordability, and quality in India.
Suggest measures to ensure inclusive adoption and effective utilization of digital health services by all sections of society.
articles.imageDescription: Infographic showing various components of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission.
articles.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) is the world's largest government-funded healthcare program.

articles.headline: India-Sri Lanka Joint Maritime Security Dialogue Concludes with Focus on Regional Stability
articles.explainer:
• India and Sri Lanka successfully concluded their joint maritime security dialogue, emphasizing enhanced cooperation in the Indian Ocean region.
• Discussions focused on combating illegal fishing, piracy, drug trafficking, and ensuring freedom of navigation.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to shared security interests and maintaining a peaceful and stable maritime environment.
• The dialogue included exchanges on intelligence sharing, joint surveillance operations, and capacity building for maritime forces.
• This engagement is crucial for reinforcing bilateral ties and addressing common challenges in the strategically important Indian Ocean.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Security challenges and their management in border areas
keyTerms: SAGAR (Security and Growth for All in the Region)
prelimsPoints: India and Sri Lanka are maritime neighbors in the Indian Ocean.
The Palk Strait and Gulf of Mannar separate the two countries.
SAGAR (Security and Growth for All in the Region) is India's vision for maritime cooperation.
Trincomalee is a strategically important port in Sri Lanka.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of maritime security cooperation between India and Sri Lanka for regional stability in the Indian Ocean.
Discuss the common challenges faced by India and Sri Lanka in the maritime domain, including illegal fishing and external influences.
Evaluate India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy in the context of its relations with Sri Lanka.
Suggest measures to further strengthen defense and security ties between the two nations.
articles.imageDescription: Naval ships of India and Sri Lanka conducting a joint exercise.
articles.trivia: Sri Lanka is home to the world's first female prime minister, Sirimavo Bandaranaike, who took office in 1960.

articles.headline: India's Green Hydrogen Mission Achieves Key Milestones, Boosts Renewable Energy Sector
articles.explainer:
• The National Green Hydrogen Mission has reported significant progress in developing green hydrogen production capabilities across India.
• Several pilot projects for green hydrogen production and utilization have commenced, attracting substantial private investment.
• The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, reducing reliance on fossil fuels.
• Focus areas include developing indigenous electrolyzer manufacturing, creating demand-side incentives, and establishing regulatory frameworks.
• This initiative is crucial for India to meet its climate change commitments and achieve energy independence.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: Green hydrogen is produced using renewable energy sources (like solar, wind) to electrolyze water.
The National Green Hydrogen Mission was launched by the Indian government.
It aims to reduce India's dependence on fossil fuel imports.
India has set a target for net-zero emissions by 2070.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate change goals.
Analyze the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and adoption in India, including technological, economic, and infrastructure hurdles.
Evaluate the potential of the National Green Hydrogen Mission to attract investment and create employment opportunities.
Suggest policy interventions and international collaborations needed to accelerate India's transition to a green hydrogen economy.
articles.imageDescription: Stylized graphic showing green hydrogen production facility with wind turbines and solar panels.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all normal matter.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite for Disaster Monitoring
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched an advanced Earth Observation Satellite (EOS) from Sriharikota.
• The satellite is designed to enhance India's capabilities in disaster monitoring, agricultural mapping, and environmental surveillance.
• It features high-resolution imaging sensors and advanced data transmission systems for real-time information.
• This launch reinforces ISRO's commitment to providing critical data for national development and disaster preparedness.
• The mission further solidifies India's position as a leader in remote sensing and space-based applications.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Space technology
prelimsPoints: ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation) is India's national space agency.
Earth Observation Satellites (EOS) are used for monitoring Earth's environment, climate, and resources.
Sriharikota in Andhra Pradesh is India's primary spaceport.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is ISRO's workhorse launch vehicle for EOS missions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the applications of Earth Observation Satellites in various sectors of national development, including disaster management, agriculture, and urban planning.
Analyze India's self-reliance in space technology and its contributions to global space exploration and applications.
Evaluate the economic and social benefits derived from ISRO's space missions for India.
Examine the challenges and future prospects for India's Earth observation program.
articles.imageDescription: A rocket launching into space from a launchpad.
articles.trivia: ISRO is one of only six government space agencies in the world that possess full launch capabilities.

articles.headline: PM-KISAN Samman Nidhi: 17th Installment Released to Farmers, Boosting Rural Economy
articles.explainer:
• The 17th installment of the Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) scheme has been disbursed to eligible farmer beneficiaries across the country.
• The scheme provides financial support directly into the bank accounts of landholding farmer families, aiming to supplement their income.
• This timely release is expected to provide crucial support to farmers for purchasing agricultural inputs and meeting other essential expenses.
• The government reiterates its commitment to farmer welfare and strengthening the rural economy through various direct benefit transfer (DBT) initiatives.
• The scheme has significantly contributed to poverty reduction and agricultural stability since its inception.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment

Major crops - cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System— objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: PM-KISAN scheme provides income support to all landholding farmer families.
The scheme was launched in December 2018.
It provides ₹6,000 per year in three equal installments.
The funds are directly transferred to the bank accounts of beneficiaries through DBT mode.
mainsPoints: Assess the impact of the PM-KISAN scheme on farmer incomes, agricultural productivity, and the overall rural economy.
Discuss the advantages and challenges of direct benefit transfer (DBT) schemes in India, particularly in the agricultural sector.
Analyze other government initiatives aimed at farmer welfare and how they complement PM-KISAN.
Suggest measures to improve the targeting and effectiveness of agricultural subsidy schemes like PM-KISAN.
articles.imageDescription: Farmers working in a field with a graphic overlay of the PM-KISAN logo.
articles.trivia: India is the world's second-largest producer of rice and wheat.

articles.headline: National Education Policy 2020 Implementation Review Highlights Progress and Challenges
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education released a review report on the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• The report highlighted significant progress in curriculum reforms, teacher training, and digital education initiatives.
• Key achievements include the development of the National Curriculum Framework (NCF) and integration of vocational education.
• Challenges remain in ensuring equitable access, addressing infrastructure gaps, and achieving desired learning outcomes across all educational levels.
• The review emphasizes the need for continued stakeholder collaboration and sustained investment to realize NEP's transformative vision.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education
prelimsPoints: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 replaced the 1986 policy.
It aims to transform India's education system from school to higher education.
Key features include a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure, multidisciplinary approach, and emphasis on vocational education.
Foundational Literacy and Numeracy (FLN) is a core focus area.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the progress and challenges in the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
Discuss how NEP 2020 aims to address issues of equity, quality, and access in the Indian education system.
Evaluate the role of digital education and technology in achieving the goals of NEP 2020, especially in post-pandemic learning.
Suggest recommendations for effective stakeholder engagement and resource mobilization to ensure successful implementation of NEP.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing educational reforms.
articles.trivia: India has one of the largest education systems in the world, with over 1.5 million schools.

articles.headline: India Solidifies Position as Global Leader in Digital Payments, UPI Drives Growth
articles.explainer:
• India continues its rapid digital transformation, emerging as a global leader in digital payment transactions.
• The Unified Payments Interface (UPI) remains the cornerstone of this growth, recording record transaction volumes monthly.
• Government initiatives like Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile (JAM) trinity and promotion of digital literacy have fueled adoption.
• The expansion of digital payments has significantly boosted financial inclusion and reduced reliance on cash transactions.
• Challenges include ensuring cybersecurity, expanding reach to remote areas, and addressing digital literacy gaps.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
prelimsPoints: UPI (Unified Payments Interface) is an instant real-time payment system developed by NPCI.
NPCI (National Payments Corporation of India) is an initiative of RBI and IBA.
The Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile (JAM) trinity is crucial for financial inclusion.
India's digital payment ecosystem is one of the largest globally by transaction volume.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors that have contributed to India's leadership in digital payments and the role of UPI in this transformation.
Discuss the impact of digital payments on financial inclusion, formalization of the economy, and governance in India.
Examine the challenges associated with the widespread adoption of digital payments, including cybersecurity, infrastructure, and digital literacy.
Suggest policy measures to further strengthen India's digital payment ecosystem and ensure its benefits reach all sections of society.
articles.imageDescription: Mobile phone displaying UPI payment interface with Indian currency symbols.
articles.trivia: India's UPI system is being adopted by other countries, showcasing its global potential.

articles.headline: Namami Gange Mission Shows Tangible Progress in Ganga Rejuvenation Efforts
articles.explainer:
• The National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) under the Namami Gange program reported significant achievements in its river rejuvenation efforts.
• Progress includes the commissioning of numerous sewage treatment plants (STPs), industrial pollution abatement, and ghat development.
• Increased public participation and enhanced monitoring systems have contributed to improved water quality in several stretches of the river.
• The mission emphasizes a holistic approach, integrating sanitation, biodiversity conservation, and sustainable river basin management.
• Despite progress, challenges persist in managing diffuse pollution sources and ensuring long-term sustainability of interventions.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: Namami Gange is an integrated conservation mission approved by the Union Government in 2014.
It aims at effective abatement of pollution and conservation and rejuvenation of the National River Ganga.
The National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) is the implementing agency.
Key pillars include sewage treatment infrastructure, industrial effluent monitoring, river surface cleaning, and biodiversity conservation.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the successes and failures of the Namami Gange Mission in achieving its objectives of Ganga rejuvenation.
Discuss the multi-faceted challenges involved in cleaning and conserving a river as vast and culturally significant as the Ganga.
Analyze the role of technology, community participation, and inter-state cooperation in river basin management.
Suggest a sustainable model for river rejuvenation that addresses both point and non-point sources of pollution.
articles.imageDescription: Clean stretch of the Ganga river with ghats and temples in the background.
articles.trivia: The Ganga river basin is the most populated river basin in the world, home to over 500 million people.

articles.headline: India's National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence: Ethics and Governance in Focus
articles.explainer:
• India's government unveiled an updated National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence (NSAI), with a strong emphasis on ethical AI and robust governance frameworks.
• The strategy aims to leverage AI for inclusive growth across sectors like healthcare, agriculture, and education.
• It prioritizes responsible AI development, data privacy, and mitigating potential biases in AI algorithms.
• The plan includes investments in AI research and development, skill enhancement, and fostering a vibrant AI innovation ecosystem.
• This forward-looking approach positions India as a leader in developing and deploying AI responsibly for societal benefit.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: Artificial Intelligence (AI) refers to the simulation of human intelligence in machines.
NITI Aayog published 'National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence' in 2018.
AI is being applied in various sectors like healthcare, agriculture, and smart cities.
Ethical considerations like bias, privacy, and accountability are crucial in AI development.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of Artificial Intelligence (AI) to drive economic growth and social development in India across various sectors.
Analyze the ethical challenges and governance issues associated with the rapid advancement and deployment of AI technologies.
Evaluate India's 'AI for All' strategy and its approach to responsible AI development, including data privacy and bias mitigation.
Suggest policy recommendations for fostering an AI-ready workforce and a robust AI innovation ecosystem in India.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract graphic of interconnected neural networks representing AI.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

articles.headline: India-US Trade Dialogue Focuses on Critical Minerals and Supply Chain Resilience
articles.explainer:
• The latest India-US Trade Policy Forum meeting saw both nations prioritizing cooperation on critical minerals and supply chain diversification.
• Discussions aimed at reducing dependence on single-source suppliers and building resilient global supply chains, particularly for advanced technologies.
• Both sides explored investment opportunities in mining, processing, and recycling of critical minerals essential for green energy and defense.
• The dialogue also addressed market access issues, intellectual property rights, and facilitating greater bilateral trade and investment.
• This strategic partnership is vital for securing critical resources and enhancing economic security for both countries.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: Critical minerals are essential for advanced technologies, renewable energy, and defense applications.
India and the US are strategic partners with growing economic ties.
Supply chain resilience became a major focus after the COVID-19 pandemic.
Lithium, Cobalt, Rare Earth Elements are examples of critical minerals.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of critical minerals for India's economic growth, technological advancement, and national security.
Discuss how India-US cooperation on critical minerals can contribute to diversifying global supply chains and reducing geopolitical risks.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in developing its domestic critical mineral processing capabilities.
Examine the broader implications of such bilateral economic dialogues for India's foreign policy and trade relations.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and USA with a graphic representation of minerals.
articles.trivia: The US is India's largest trading partner in goods.

articles.headline: Government Launches New Initiative for Climate-Resilient Agriculture
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare launched a new national initiative focused on promoting climate-resilient agriculture practices.
• The program aims to equip farmers with techniques and technologies to mitigate the impacts of climate change, such as droughts and floods.
• Key components include developing climate-smart crop varieties, water-efficient irrigation, and sustainable soil management practices.
• The initiative emphasizes research, extension services, and financial support for farmers adopting resilient farming methods.
• This move is critical for ensuring food security and sustaining agricultural livelihoods in the face of environmental challenges.
gsSubTopics: Major crops - cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Food security
prelimsPoints: Climate-resilient agriculture aims to adapt agricultural systems to climate change impacts.
Droughts, floods, and extreme weather events severely affect Indian agriculture.
The National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture (NICRA) is an existing initiative.
Crop diversification and water conservation are key strategies.
mainsPoints: Discuss the vulnerabilities of Indian agriculture to climate change and the imperative for adopting climate-resilient practices.
Analyze the components of the new initiative for climate-resilient agriculture and its potential impact on farmer incomes and food security.
Evaluate the role of technology, research, and extension services in promoting sustainable agricultural practices in India.
Suggest policy measures to incentivize farmers to adopt climate-resilient farming methods and integrate traditional knowledge.
articles.imageDescription: Farmers inspecting healthy crops in a field, with advanced irrigation systems visible.
articles.trivia: India is one of the world's largest agricultural producers, with agriculture contributing significantly to its GDP and employment.

articles.headline: NITI Aayog Report Calls for Enhanced Financial Autonomy for Urban Local Bodies
articles.explainer:
• A recent report by NITI Aayog advocated for greater financial autonomy and resource mobilization for Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) in India.
• The report highlighted the critical role of ULBs in urban governance, infrastructure development, and service delivery, which are often hampered by financial constraints.
• Recommendations include increasing own-source revenues, strengthening property tax collection, and better utilization of municipal bonds.
• It also suggested improving inter-governmental fiscal transfers and capacity building for ULB officials.
• Empowering ULBs financially is crucial for sustainable urban development and achieving the goals of smart cities.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog is a policy think tank of the Government of India.
Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) are constitutional bodies established under the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act.
Their functions include urban planning, public health, sanitation, and infrastructure provision.
Property tax is a major source of own-source revenue for ULBs.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges faced by Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) in India regarding financial autonomy and effective governance.
Analyze the recommendations of the NITI Aayog report for strengthening municipal finance and improving service delivery in urban areas.
Evaluate the role of the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act in empowering ULBs and the gaps in its implementation.
Suggest a roadmap for sustainable urban development by enhancing the capacity and financial independence of ULBs.
articles.imageDescription: Modern cityscape with well-maintained public infrastructure.
articles.trivia: Mumbai's Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC) is one of the richest municipal corporations in India.

articles.headline: Indus Waters Treaty: Permanent Indus Commission Meets to Address Water Sharing Issues
articles.explainer:
• The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) comprising water commissioners from India and Pakistan held its annual meeting.
• The discussions focused on various technical aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), including data exchange and hydroelectric projects.
• Both sides reiterated their commitment to upholding the provisions of the IWT, a crucial agreement for water sharing in the region.
• The meeting aimed to resolve outstanding issues and ensure smooth implementation of the treaty amidst evolving hydrological conditions.
• This dialogue underscores the importance of continued cooperation on shared water resources despite broader geopolitical tensions.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Water resource management
prelimsPoints: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) was signed between India and Pakistan in 1960, brokered by the World Bank.
The treaty allocates the waters of the Indus River system between the two countries.
India has exclusive use of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej).
Pakistan has exclusive use of the Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab).
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) meets annually to resolve issues.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical significance and continued relevance of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) in India-Pakistan relations.
Discuss the challenges in implementing the IWT, particularly concerning hydroelectric projects and climate change impacts on water flow.
Evaluate the role of water diplomacy in fostering cooperation between riparian states, even amidst political tensions.
Suggest measures to strengthen the framework of the IWT to address future water management challenges in the Indus basin.
articles.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River system and its tributaries flowing through India and Pakistan.
articles.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty is considered one of the most successful water-sharing treaties in the world, having survived multiple conflicts between India and Pakistan.

articles.headline: Conservation Efforts Intensify for Critically Endangered Great Indian Bustard
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change announced intensified conservation efforts for the critically endangered Great Indian Bustard (GIB).
• New measures include expanding protected habitats, implementing underground power lines, and increasing community involvement.
• The GIB population has dwindled drastically due to habitat loss, poaching, and collision with power lines.
• The Supreme Court has also intervened, emphasizing the need to protect the species and its habitat.
• These urgent actions are vital to prevent the extinction of this iconic bird and preserve India's biodiversity.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Biodiversity and its conservation
prelimsPoints: The Great Indian Bustard (GIB) is a critically endangered bird species.
It is found primarily in Rajasthan and Gujarat in India.
Its largest population is in the Desert National Park, Rajasthan.
The GIB is listed in Schedule I of the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.
Project Great Indian Bustard is a state-led conservation initiative.
mainsPoints: Discuss the major threats leading to the critically endangered status of the Great Indian Bustard (GIB) in India.
Analyze the effectiveness of current conservation strategies and the role of legal interventions (e.g., Supreme Court directives) in protecting the GIB.
Evaluate the importance of habitat restoration, community engagement, and technological solutions in species recovery programs.
Suggest a comprehensive, multi-stakeholder approach for the long-term survival of the Great Indian Bustard and other endangered species.
articles.imageDescription: Photograph of a Great Indian Bustard in its natural habitat in a grassland.
articles.trivia: The Great Indian Bustard is one of the heaviest flying birds in the world.

highlights: Group Captain Shubhanshu Shukla becomes the second Indian to go to space aboard Axiom Mission 4, highlighting India's growing space capabilities.
Himachal Pradesh faces devastating flash floods, claiming lives and leaving many missing, underscoring climate change vulnerability.
Thirty-five Nepali youths rescued from captivity in Uttarakhand in a joint operation, exposing human trafficking issues.
Supreme Court directs review of the Electoral Bond Scheme, raising concerns over transparency in political funding.
India and Japan deepen strategic ties, focusing on Indo-Pacific security and critical technologies.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the recent space mission involving an Indian astronaut, consider the following statements:
1. Group Captain Shubhanshu Shukla is the first Indian citizen to go to space.
2. He participated in the Axiom Mission 4, a private spaceflight to the International Space Station (ISS).
3. The mission launched from the Kennedy Space Center in the United States.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect. Group Captain Shubhanshu Shukla is the *second* Indian citizen to go to space, after Rakesh Sharma in 1984. Statements 2 and 3 are correct, as he was part of Axiom Mission 4, a private spaceflight to the ISS, launched from Kennedy Space Center.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements best describes 'Green Hydrogen'?
(a) Hydrogen produced from natural gas with carbon capture and storage.
(b) Hydrogen produced through electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.
(c) Hydrogen produced from coal gasification.
(d) Hydrogen produced as a byproduct of industrial chemical processes.
options: (a) Hydrogen produced from natural gas with carbon capture and storage.
(b) Hydrogen produced through electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.
(d) Hydrogen produced as a byproduct of industrial chemical processes.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) Hydrogen produced through electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.
prelimsMocks.explanation: Green hydrogen specifically refers to hydrogen produced through the electrolysis of water, powered by renewable electricity sources such as solar or wind, resulting in minimal or zero greenhouse gas emissions.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following about the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT):
1. It was signed between India and Pakistan in 1960.
2. The treaty was brokered by the United Nations.
3. India has exclusive use of the waters of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The Indus Waters Treaty was signed in 1960. Statement 2 is incorrect. The treaty was brokered by the World Bank, not the United Nations. Statement 3 is correct. India has exclusive use of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej), while Pakistan has exclusive use of the Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab).

prelimsMocks.question:
The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) has been renamed as:
(a) Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PMJAY)
(b) Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM)
(c) National Health Protection Scheme (NHPS)
(d) Digital India Health Initiative (DIHI)
prelimsMocks.explanation: The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) was renamed Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to align it more closely with the broader Ayushman Bharat program, aiming to create an integrated digital health infrastructure.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following is NOT a primary objective of the Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) monetary policy in India?
(a) Maintaining price stability.
(b) Ensuring adequate flow of credit to productive sectors.
(c) Promoting economic growth.
(d) Directly managing government expenditure.
options: (b) Ensuring adequate flow of credit to productive sectors.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The primary objectives of RBI's monetary policy are price stability, ensuring credit flow, and promoting economic growth. Directly managing government expenditure falls under fiscal policy, which is handled by the Ministry of Finance, not the RBI.

mainsMocks.question: Discuss the significance of India's growing participation in international space missions for its indigenous space program. Analyze the opportunities and challenges posed by private sector involvement in global space exploration.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Science and Technology—developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Space technology.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce Shubhanshu Shukla's mission as a significant milestone. Discuss how international collaborations (like Axiom) enhance India's technological capabilities, human spaceflight experience, and geopolitical standing. Highlight opportunities such as technology transfer, cost-sharing, and market access. Address challenges like intellectual property rights, dependency on foreign technology, and the need for robust regulatory frameworks for private players. Conclude with India's vision for a self-reliant and collaborative space sector.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the efficacy of the Electoral Bond Scheme in promoting transparent political funding in India. In light of recent Supreme Court directives, suggest comprehensive reforms to ensure greater accountability in electoral finance.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure; Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act; Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
mainsMocks.approach: Begin by explaining the Electoral Bond Scheme's stated objectives and its current form. Critically evaluate its impact on transparency, highlighting arguments about anonymity and potential for quid pro quo. Refer to the Supreme Court's concerns. Suggest reforms such as mandatory disclosure of donor identities, strengthening the Election Commission's powers, public funding of elections, and stricter regulations on corporate donations. Conclude by emphasizing the importance of transparent political funding for a healthy democracy.

mainsMocks.question: The increasing frequency and intensity of flash floods in the Himalayan region pose significant challenges to disaster management and sustainable development. Analyze the underlying causes of such events in Himachal Pradesh and propose a multi-pronged strategy for disaster risk reduction and resilience building.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS1: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes. GS3: Disaster and disaster management.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the context of Himachal Pradesh flash floods. Analyze causes: natural (heavy rainfall, geological vulnerability, glacial lake outburst floods, climate change) and anthropogenic (unplanned infrastructure development, deforestation, altered river courses). Propose a multi-pronged strategy: early warning systems (Doppler radars), resilient infrastructure, reforestation, community-based disaster management, strict land-use planning, inter-state cooperation for river basin management, and climate change adaptation measures. Conclude with the need for a holistic and proactive approach.

quickBytes.text: Shubhanshu Shukla is the second Indian citizen to travel to space.
The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) was signed in 1960 between India and Pakistan, brokered by the World Bank.
Green Hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy, emitting zero carbon.
The 74th Constitutional Amendment Act deals with Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) in India.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) of the RBI sets the benchmark repo rate.
The Great Indian Bustard (GIB) is a critically endangered species, primarily found in Rajasthan and Gujarat.
Unified Payments Interface (UPI) is an instant real-time payment system developed by NPCI.
The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 replaced the 1986 education policy.
The National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) implements the Namami Gange program.
India and Japan are key partners in the Quad grouping, focusing on Indo-Pacific security.

GS1.headline: Devastating Flash Floods in Himachal Pradesh Claim Lives, Leave Many Missing
GS1.explainer:
• Heavy rainfall triggered severe flash floods and landslides across several districts of Himachal Pradesh.
• At least three fatalities have been confirmed, with rescue operations underway for 11 individuals reported missing.
• The floods have caused extensive damage to infrastructure, including roads, bridges, and residential areas, disrupting normal life.
• Authorities have issued high alert warnings, advising residents and tourists to avoid vulnerable areas and initiating relief measures.
• The incident highlights the increasing vulnerability of the Himalayan region to extreme weather events, exacerbated by climate change and unplanned development.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena
Disaster and disaster management
Changes in critical geographical features
prelimsPoints: Flash floods are characterized by their rapid onset and short duration, often caused by intense rainfall.
Himachal Pradesh is part of the Western Himalayas, prone to geological instability and heavy monsoonal rains.
The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) is the apex body for disaster management in India.
NDRF (National Disaster Response Force) plays a crucial role in search and rescue operations.
mainsPoints: Analyze the causes of increasing frequency and intensity of flash floods in the Himalayan region, including climate change and anthropogenic factors.
Discuss the challenges in disaster preparedness and response in mountainous terrains like Himachal Pradesh.
Suggest comprehensive strategies for disaster risk reduction and mitigation in vulnerable regions, focusing on early warning systems, infrastructure resilience, and community participation.
Evaluate the role of land-use planning and environmental regulations in preventing such disasters.
GS1.imageDescription: Satellite image showing heavy cloud cover over the Himalayan region, indicative of monsoonal activity.
GS1.trivia: Himachal Pradesh is often referred to as 'Dev Bhoomi' (Land of Gods) due to its numerous ancient temples and picturesque natural beauty.

GS2.headline: Thirty-Five Nepali Youths Rescued from Captivity in Uttarakhand
GS2.explainer:
• A joint operation by the Nepali Embassy and Indian authorities successfully rescued 35 Nepali youths from captivity in Kashipur, Uttarakhand.
• The youths were reportedly held under exploitative conditions, highlighting issues of human trafficking and illegal labor practices.
• The coordinated effort demonstrates strong bilateral cooperation between India and Nepal on humanitarian and security matters.
• Investigations are ongoing to apprehend the perpetrators and dismantle the human trafficking network responsible.
• This incident underscores the need for enhanced cross-border vigilance and legal frameworks to combat human trafficking and protect vulnerable migrant workers.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood relations
Internal security challenges
Social justice and protection of vulnerable sections
keyTerms: Schengen Area (for comparison of open borders)
prelimsPoints: India and Nepal share an open border, facilitating movement but also posing challenges for security.
Human trafficking is a transnational organized crime.
The Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) coordinates anti-human trafficking efforts in India.
The Immoral Traffic (Prevention) Act, 1956 (ITPA) is the primary legislation against trafficking for sexual exploitation.
mainsPoints: Examine the socio-economic factors that make Nepali youths vulnerable to human trafficking in India.
Discuss the challenges and successes of India-Nepal bilateral cooperation in combating cross-border crimes like human trafficking.
Suggest policy measures and institutional reforms required to strengthen anti-human trafficking efforts in India, focusing on prevention, protection, and prosecution.
Analyze the role of international organizations and NGOs in addressing human trafficking in South Asia.
GS2.imageDescription: Image of Indian and Nepali flags intertwined, symbolizing bilateral cooperation.
GS2.trivia: The India-Nepal border is one of the most open international borders in the world, allowing free movement of people.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Directs Review of Electoral Bond Scheme, Citing Transparency Concerns
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India has issued directives to the central government for a comprehensive review of the Electoral Bond Scheme.
• The court expressed concerns regarding the scheme's opacity and its potential impact on electoral transparency and fair political funding.
• This ruling comes amidst ongoing debates about the anonymity of political donations and the need for greater accountability in party funding.
• The government has been asked to consider amendments that ensure donor identity disclosure while balancing privacy concerns.
• The decision is a significant step towards reinforcing democratic principles and ensuring a level playing field in elections.
gsSubTopics: Separation of powers
Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
prelimsPoints: Electoral Bonds were introduced in 2017 to provide a mechanism for political funding.
The scheme allows individuals and corporate entities to donate to political parties anonymously.
The Supreme Court had previously heard petitions challenging the scheme's constitutionality.
The Election Commission of India (ECI) has also raised concerns about electoral transparency.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the Electoral Bond Scheme in light of principles of transparency, accountability, and free and fair elections.
Discuss the arguments for and against anonymous political donations in a democratic setup.
Analyze the role of the Supreme Court as a guardian of the Constitution in electoral matters.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for political funding that promote transparency while ensuring ease of donation.
GS2.imageDescription: Gavel and Indian Constitution book on a courtroom desk.
GS2.trivia: The first general elections in India in 1951-52 were conducted over several months due to logistical challenges.

GS2.headline: India and Japan Deepen Strategic Ties with Focus on Indo-Pacific Security
GS2.explainer:
• India and Japan held their annual Strategic Dialogue in New Delhi, reaffirming their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defense cooperation, maritime security, and regional stability in the face of evolving geopolitical challenges.
• Both nations emphasized collaboration on critical and emerging technologies, supply chain resilience, and infrastructure development in the region.
• The dialogue also touched upon multilateral cooperation within forums like the Quad and G20, strengthening their comprehensive strategic partnership.
• This engagement reflects a concerted effort to counter regional assertiveness and promote a rules-based international order.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India and Japan are members of the Quad grouping, along with the USA and Australia.
Japan is a key partner in India's Act East Policy.
Both countries share concerns regarding maritime security in the Indo-Pacific.
Defense exercises like 'Dharma Guardian' and 'JIMEX' are conducted between India and Japan.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic convergence between India and Japan in the Indo-Pacific region and its implications for regional security.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in deepening defense and economic cooperation between India and Japan.
Evaluate the role of bilateral and multilateral forums like Quad in shaping the geopolitical landscape of the Indo-Pacific.
Examine how India's 'Act East' policy aligns with Japan's strategic interests in the region.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan displayed together in a formal setting.
GS2.trivia: Japan is a major investor in India's infrastructure projects, including the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail corridor.

GS2.headline: National Digital Health Mission Expands Reach, Focuses on Tier-2 and Tier-3 Cities
GS2.explainer:
• The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM), now known as Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM), announced its expanded rollout to Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities.
• The expansion aims to bridge the healthcare accessibility gap by leveraging digital technologies for health records, teleconsultations, and seamless service delivery.
• Key components include Health ID generation, Healthcare Professionals Registry (HPR), and Health Facility Registry (HFR).
• The initiative seeks to create an integrated digital health infrastructure, improving efficiency and transparency in the healthcare system.
• Challenges remain in ensuring digital literacy, data privacy, and interoperability across diverse healthcare providers.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Development processes and the development industry—the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
prelimsPoints: The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) was renamed Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM).
It aims to create a national digital health ecosystem.
Key features include Health ID, Health Facility Registry, and Healthcare Professionals Registry.
The mission is under the National Health Authority (NHA).
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to transform healthcare delivery in India, especially in remote and underserved areas.
Analyze the challenges involved in implementing a nationwide digital health infrastructure, including data privacy, digital divide, and cybersecurity.
Evaluate the role of technology in improving healthcare accessibility, affordability, and quality in India.
Suggest measures to ensure inclusive adoption and effective utilization of digital health services by all sections of society.
GS2.imageDescription: Infographic showing various components of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission.
GS2.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) is the world's largest government-funded healthcare program.

GS2.headline: India-Sri Lanka Joint Maritime Security Dialogue Concludes with Focus on Regional Stability
GS2.explainer:
• India and Sri Lanka successfully concluded their joint maritime security dialogue, emphasizing enhanced cooperation in the Indian Ocean region.
• Discussions focused on combating illegal fishing, piracy, drug trafficking, and ensuring freedom of navigation.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to shared security interests and maintaining a peaceful and stable maritime environment.
• The dialogue included exchanges on intelligence sharing, joint surveillance operations, and capacity building for maritime forces.
• This engagement is crucial for reinforcing bilateral ties and addressing common challenges in the strategically important Indian Ocean.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Security challenges and their management in border areas
keyTerms: SAGAR (Security and Growth for All in the Region)
prelimsPoints: India and Sri Lanka are maritime neighbors in the Indian Ocean.
The Palk Strait and Gulf of Mannar separate the two countries.
SAGAR (Security and Growth for All in the Region) is India's vision for maritime cooperation.
Trincomalee is a strategically important port in Sri Lanka.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of maritime security cooperation between India and Sri Lanka for regional stability in the Indian Ocean.
Discuss the common challenges faced by India and Sri Lanka in the maritime domain, including illegal fishing and external influences.
Evaluate India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy in the context of its relations with Sri Lanka.
Suggest measures to further strengthen defense and security ties between the two nations.
GS2.imageDescription: Naval ships of India and Sri Lanka conducting a joint exercise.
GS2.trivia: Sri Lanka is home to the world's first female prime minister, Sirimavo Bandaranaike, who took office in 1960.

GS2.headline: National Education Policy 2020 Implementation Review Highlights Progress and Challenges
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education released a review report on the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• The report highlighted significant progress in curriculum reforms, teacher training, and digital education initiatives.
• Key achievements include the development of the National Curriculum Framework (NCF) and integration of vocational education.
• Challenges remain in ensuring equitable access, addressing infrastructure gaps, and achieving desired learning outcomes across all educational levels.
• The review emphasizes the need for continued stakeholder collaboration and sustained investment to realize NEP's transformative vision.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education
prelimsPoints: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 replaced the 1986 policy.
It aims to transform India's education system from school to higher education.
Key features include a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure, multidisciplinary approach, and emphasis on vocational education.
Foundational Literacy and Numeracy (FLN) is a core focus area.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the progress and challenges in the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
Discuss how NEP 2020 aims to address issues of equity, quality, and access in the Indian education system.
Evaluate the role of digital education and technology in achieving the goals of NEP 2020, especially in post-pandemic learning.
Suggest recommendations for effective stakeholder engagement and resource mobilization to ensure successful implementation of NEP.
GS2.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing educational reforms.
GS2.trivia: India has one of the largest education systems in the world, with over 1.5 million schools.

GS2.headline: India-US Trade Dialogue Focuses on Critical Minerals and Supply Chain Resilience
GS2.explainer:
• The latest India-US Trade Policy Forum meeting saw both nations prioritizing cooperation on critical minerals and supply chain diversification.
• Discussions aimed at reducing dependence on single-source suppliers and building resilient global supply chains, particularly for advanced technologies.
• Both sides explored investment opportunities in mining, processing, and recycling of critical minerals essential for green energy and defense.
• The dialogue also addressed market access issues, intellectual property rights, and facilitating greater bilateral trade and investment.
• This strategic partnership is vital for securing critical resources and enhancing economic security for both countries.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: Critical minerals are essential for advanced technologies, renewable energy, and defense applications.
India and the US are strategic partners with growing economic ties.
Supply chain resilience became a major focus after the COVID-19 pandemic.
Lithium, Cobalt, Rare Earth Elements are examples of critical minerals.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of critical minerals for India's economic growth, technological advancement, and national security.
Discuss how India-US cooperation on critical minerals can contribute to diversifying global supply chains and reducing geopolitical risks.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in developing its domestic critical mineral processing capabilities.
Examine the broader implications of such bilateral economic dialogues for India's foreign policy and trade relations.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and USA with a graphic representation of minerals.
GS2.trivia: The US is India's largest trading partner in goods.

GS2.headline: NITI Aayog Report Calls for Enhanced Financial Autonomy for Urban Local Bodies
GS2.explainer:
• A recent report by NITI Aayog advocated for greater financial autonomy and resource mobilization for Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) in India.
• The report highlighted the critical role of ULBs in urban governance, infrastructure development, and service delivery, which are often hampered by financial constraints.
• Recommendations include increasing own-source revenues, strengthening property tax collection, and better utilization of municipal bonds.
• It also suggested improving inter-governmental fiscal transfers and capacity building for ULB officials.
• Empowering ULBs financially is crucial for sustainable urban development and achieving the goals of smart cities.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog is a policy think tank of the Government of India.
Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) are constitutional bodies established under the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act.
Their functions include urban planning, public health, sanitation, and infrastructure provision.
Property tax is a major source of own-source revenue for ULBs.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges faced by Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) in India regarding financial autonomy and effective governance.
Analyze the recommendations of the NITI Aayog report for strengthening municipal finance and improving service delivery in urban areas.
Evaluate the role of the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act in empowering ULBs and the gaps in its implementation.
Suggest a roadmap for sustainable urban development by enhancing the capacity and financial independence of ULBs.
GS2.imageDescription: Modern cityscape with well-maintained public infrastructure.
GS2.trivia: Mumbai's Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC) is one of the richest municipal corporations in India.

GS2.headline: Indus Waters Treaty: Permanent Indus Commission Meets to Address Water Sharing Issues
GS2.explainer:
• The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) comprising water commissioners from India and Pakistan held its annual meeting.
• The discussions focused on various technical aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), including data exchange and hydroelectric projects.
• Both sides reiterated their commitment to upholding the provisions of the IWT, a crucial agreement for water sharing in the region.
• The meeting aimed to resolve outstanding issues and ensure smooth implementation of the treaty amidst evolving hydrological conditions.
• This dialogue underscores the importance of continued cooperation on shared water resources despite broader geopolitical tensions.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Water resource management
prelimsPoints: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) was signed between India and Pakistan in 1960, brokered by the World Bank.
The treaty allocates the waters of the Indus River system between the two countries.
India has exclusive use of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej).
Pakistan has exclusive use of the Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab).
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) meets annually to resolve issues.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical significance and continued relevance of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) in India-Pakistan relations.
Discuss the challenges in implementing the IWT, particularly concerning hydroelectric projects and climate change impacts on water flow.
Evaluate the role of water diplomacy in fostering cooperation between riparian states, even amidst political tensions.
Suggest measures to strengthen the framework of the IWT to address future water management challenges in the Indus basin.
GS2.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River system and its tributaries flowing through India and Pakistan.
GS2.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty is considered one of the most successful water-sharing treaties in the world, having survived multiple conflicts between India and Pakistan.

GS3.headline: Group Captain Shubhanshu Shukla Becomes Second Indian in Space Aboard Axiom Mission 4
GS3.explainer:
• Group Captain Shubhanshu Shukla made history as the second Indian citizen to journey into outer space, after Rakesh Sharma in 1984.
• He served as a mission specialist aboard Axiom Mission 4 (Ax-4), a private spaceflight to the International Space Station (ISS).
• The mission, which includes astronauts from other nations, successfully launched from the Kennedy Space Center in the United States.
• Shukla's participation underscores India's growing collaboration in international space ventures and its aspiration to send Indian astronauts on future missions.
• This achievement highlights India's progress in space technology and human spaceflight capabilities, aligning with the Gaganyaan mission objectives.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Space technology
Awareness in the fields of Space
prelimsPoints: Shubhanshu Shukla is the second Indian to go to space (first was Rakesh Sharma in 1984).
He flew as a Group Captain on Axiom Mission 4 (Ax-4).
Ax-4 is a private mission to the International Space Station (ISS).
The launch took place from Kennedy Space Center, USA.
Axiom Space is a private company facilitating commercial space missions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's participation in international commercial space missions for its own space program (Gaganyaan).
Analyze the role of private sector in global space exploration and India's potential.
Evaluate the geopolitical implications of India's growing presence in space, including collaboration and competition.
Examine the scientific and technological spin-offs from human spaceflight missions for India.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of an astronaut with the Indian flag on a spacesuit, with a rocket launching in the background.
GS3.trivia: The first Indian-origin person to go to space was Kalpana Chawla, but Rakesh Sharma was the first Indian citizen. Shubhanshu Shukla is the second Indian citizen.

GS3.headline: RBI Holds Repo Rate Steady Amidst Persistent Inflationary Pressures
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) decided to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged at its current level.
• This decision reflects the RBI's cautious approach, prioritizing inflation control despite calls for economic growth stimulus.
• The MPC highlighted persistent inflationary pressures, particularly from food prices and global commodity markets, as key concerns.
• The central bank reaffirmed its commitment to withdrawing accommodation gradually to ensure inflation aligns with the target band.
• Analysts anticipate continued vigilance from the RBI, with future rate decisions dependent on evolving inflation trajectory and global economic conditions.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary policy
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is responsible for setting the benchmark interest rate (repo rate) in India.
The primary objective of the RBI's monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation targeting framework in India aims for CPI inflation of 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
mainsPoints: Analyze the dilemma faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and economic growth in the current global and domestic scenario.
Discuss the effectiveness of monetary policy tools, particularly the repo rate, in managing inflationary pressures in India.
Evaluate the impact of global commodity prices and supply chain disruptions on India's domestic inflation.
Examine the challenges in achieving the inflation target and propose measures for better coordination between fiscal and monetary policies.
GS3.imageDescription: RBI logo with Indian currency notes in the background.
GS3.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

GS3.headline: India's Green Hydrogen Mission Achieves Key Milestones, Boosts Renewable Energy Sector
GS3.explainer:
• The National Green Hydrogen Mission has reported significant progress in developing green hydrogen production capabilities across India.
• Several pilot projects for green hydrogen production and utilization have commenced, attracting substantial private investment.
• The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, reducing reliance on fossil fuels.
• Focus areas include developing indigenous electrolyzer manufacturing, creating demand-side incentives, and establishing regulatory frameworks.
• This initiative is crucial for India to meet its climate change commitments and achieve energy independence.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: Green hydrogen is produced using renewable energy sources (like solar, wind) to electrolyze water.
The National Green Hydrogen Mission was launched by the Indian government.
It aims to reduce India's dependence on fossil fuel imports.
India has set a target for net-zero emissions by 2070.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate change goals.
Analyze the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and adoption in India, including technological, economic, and infrastructure hurdles.
Evaluate the potential of the National Green Hydrogen Mission to attract investment and create employment opportunities.
Suggest policy interventions and international collaborations needed to accelerate India's transition to a green hydrogen economy.
GS3.imageDescription: Stylized graphic showing green hydrogen production facility with wind turbines and solar panels.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all normal matter.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite for Disaster Monitoring
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched an advanced Earth Observation Satellite (EOS) from Sriharikota.
• The satellite is designed to enhance India's capabilities in disaster monitoring, agricultural mapping, and environmental surveillance.
• It features high-resolution imaging sensors and advanced data transmission systems for real-time information.
• This launch reinforces ISRO's commitment to providing critical data for national development and disaster preparedness.
• The mission further solidifies India's position as a leader in remote sensing and space-based applications.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Space technology
prelimsPoints: ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation) is India's national space agency.
Earth Observation Satellites (EOS) are used for monitoring Earth's environment, climate, and resources.
Sriharikota in Andhra Pradesh is India's primary spaceport.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is ISRO's workhorse launch vehicle for EOS missions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the applications of Earth Observation Satellites in various sectors of national development, including disaster management, agriculture, and urban planning.
Analyze India's self-reliance in space technology and its contributions to global space exploration and applications.
